name: github
context: GitHub platform ecosystem — repos, issues, PRs, actions, webhooks, and API automation
info: The GitHub ecosystem covers repository management, issue tracking, pull request workflows, CI/CD via GitHub Actions, webhook-driven automation, and REST/GraphQL API integration. This tree is organized around the jobs an agent actually performs: creating and configuring repositories, filing and triaging issues, opening and merging pull requests, triggering CI/CD pipelines, and reacting to webhook events. Octokit is the primary SDK family. Authentication spans personal access tokens, GitHub Apps, and OAuth flows. All operations target the GitHub REST API v3 or GraphQL API v4.
body:
# GitHub Ecosystem

GitHub is the world's largest code hosting platform where agents manage repositories, track issues, review and merge pull requests, orchestrate CI/CD pipelines, and respond to webhook events. The GitHub API (REST v3 and GraphQL v4) powers all programmatic interactions.

## Agent Jobs

The primary tasks an agent performs in this ecosystem:

- **Manage repos** — create, clone, configure, and administer GitHub repositories. See [[github/repos]].
- **Create issues** — file bugs, feature requests, and tasks with labels, assignees, and milestones. See [[github/issues]].
- **Open PRs** — create, review, approve, and merge pull requests across branches and forks. See [[github/pull-requests]].
- **Run actions** — configure and trigger GitHub Actions CI/CD workflows for testing, building, and deploying. See [[github/actions]].
- **Handle webhooks** — set up event-driven automation that reacts to pushes, PRs, issues, and other repository events. See [[github/webhooks]].

## API Surface

GitHub exposes two API interfaces:

| API | Endpoint | Use Case |
|-----|----------|----------|
| REST v3 | `https://api.github.com` | CRUD operations, simple queries |
| GraphQL v4 | `https://api.github.com/graphql` | Complex queries, nested data, mutations |

## Authentication

All API calls require authentication. See [[github/auth]] for details on:

- **Personal Access Tokens (PATs)** — simplest method, scoped to a user
- **GitHub Apps** — machine-to-machine, fine-grained permissions, installed on repos/orgs
- **OAuth Apps** — user-authorized, for third-party integrations
- **Fine-grained tokens** — repository-scoped PATs with granular permission sets

## Libraries

- **@octokit/rest** — full-featured Node.js REST client
- **@octokit/graphql** — GraphQL client for complex queries
- **@octokit/webhooks** — webhook payload verification and typing
- **@octokit/auth-app** — GitHub App authentication
- **gh CLI** — official GitHub CLI for terminal workflows
- **PyGithub** — Python GitHub API wrapper

sections.title: API overview
sections.body: GitHub provides REST v3 (`https://api.github.com`) for standard CRUD and GraphQL v4 (`https://api.github.com/graphql`) for complex nested queries. REST is simpler for single-resource operations. GraphQL reduces round-trips when you need data from multiple related resources. Both require authentication via tokens or GitHub App credentials. See [[github/auth]] for authentication setup.

sections.title: Authentication methods
sections.body: Personal Access Tokens (classic and fine-grained) are the simplest auth method. GitHub Apps provide machine-to-machine auth with installation tokens. OAuth flows authorize third-party apps on behalf of users. Fine-grained tokens scope access to specific repositories and permissions. See [[github/auth]] for detailed setup of each method.

sections.title: Rate limits
sections.body: Authenticated REST requests: 5,000/hour. GitHub App installation tokens: 5,000/hour per installation. GraphQL: 5,000 points/hour. Unauthenticated: 60/hour. Use conditional requests (`If-None-Match`, `If-Modified-Since`) to avoid consuming rate limit on unchanged resources. Monitor via `X-RateLimit-Remaining` response header.
